source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 9.5 Process management (pp. 161-163)

questions.q: Why is total processing time divided into small ranges shared between processes?
options: So processes take turns on the processor, enabling multitasking rather than one process hogging it
Because the CPU can only store one process at a time
To save electricity
Because processes cannot have priorities
questions.why: Time-sharing gives each prioritised process a slice of processor time in turn, which is what makes multitasking possible.

questions.q: Which of these is the 'zombie' process state?
options: A request for destruction (terminated but not yet reaped)
Waiting for the processor to become available
Currently accessing the processor
Waiting for an I/O operation
questions.why: Among the states (ready, in execution, suspended, stopped, zombie, dead), zombie is the 'request for destruction' state.

questions.q: What happens to child processes when their parent dies?
options: They become orphans and are adopted by the init process, which will destroy them
They are killed instantly
They become the new init
They keep running with no parent forever
questions.why: Orphaned children are adopted by init, which cleans them up.

questions.q: What is the nice value range, and the default priority of a process?
options: Nice ranges from -20 to 19; the default priority is 0
Nice ranges from 0 to 99; default is 50
Nice ranges from -99 to 99; default is 20
Nice ranges from 100 to 139; default is 100
questions.why: Nice adjusts an ordinary process's priority over -20..19 (default 0); real-time processes use 0-99 and ordinary ones 100-139 internally.

questions.q: What defines SYNCHRONOUS execution of a command?
options: The user loses the shell until the command finishes, then the prompt returns
The command runs in the background and the prompt returns at once
The command runs on another terminal
The command never returns a prompt
questions.why: Synchronous means you wait: the prompt only reappears when the process ends. Asynchronous (background) returns the prompt immediately.

questions.q: Which is a constraint of running a command asynchronously (in the background)?
options: It must not wait for keyboard input or write results to the screen, and quitting the shell ends it
It must always print to the screen
It cannot have a priority
It must be run by root
questions.why: A background job should take no keyboard input and produce no screen output; leaving the shell terminates it (see nohup to avoid that).
